worth_it_explanation: Block lists decide which advertisers, categories, and domains are kept off a publisher's inventory, so they protect the brand the publisher has built and the trust of the audience that reads it. A block list that is thin lets unwanted creatives through, while one that is over-broad suppresses demand the publisher meant to keep. The check is a review of the blocking controls a publisher already routes through Amazon Publisher Services, so it costs reading time rather than rebuild time, and the finding points to a concrete list the publisher can tighten or relax.
intro: Creative blocking decides which advertisers, ad categories, and creative attributes are allowed to clear on a publisher's inventory through Amazon Publisher Services (APS). Because APS is a server-side header-bidding suite, the blocking signals a publisher sets flow into the bid requests APS sends to demand partners, where they line up with the OpenRTB blocking fields the standard already defines (e.g., blocked advertiser domains and blocked content categories). This subcategory is about whether those block lists match the publisher's own standards and whether they are scoped so they protect the brand without needlessly cutting off demand. These checks confirm that the blocking controls are a deliberate reflection of what the publisher will and will not run, rather than an empty default or a list grown so wide that revenue leaks out of it.

audit_checks.title: Advertiser, category, and domain block lists do not match the publisher's standards
audit_checks.impact_rationale: Block lists are the control that keeps unwanted advertisers and ad categories off a publisher's inventory. When the lists are empty, stale, or never reconciled against what the publisher actually refuses to run, creatives the publisher would reject can clear in the auction, which puts the brand and the audience's trust at risk on inventory the publisher cannot easily claw back after the fact.

educate.base:
Amazon Publisher Services (APS) runs as a server-side header-bidding suite, so when a publisher sets which advertisers and ad categories to keep off its inventory, that intent travels into the bid requests APS sends out to demand partners. The OpenRTB standard that those requests follow carries dedicated blocking fields, e.g., a block list of advertiser domains (badv) and a list of blocked content categories (bcat) drawn from the IAB content categories. A publisher's brand-safety standards are only enforced to the extent those signals are populated and kept current.

The audit question is whether the block lists match what the publisher will actually refuse to run. A media brand may bar competitors, regulated categories such as gambling or alcohol, or categories that clash with its editorial stance. A list that was set up once and never revisited drifts away from that standard as the publisher's positions change, as new advertiser domains appear, and as the IAB content taxonomy itself is revised. The IAB Tech Lab has moved publishers and vendors onto Content Taxonomy 3.x, so a category block list still keyed to a deprecated 1.x or 2.x version can map categories differently than the publisher intends.

There is also a question of where the list lives. A publisher running APS often runs other demand paths alongside it, e.g., a client-side Prebid wrapper or a direct ad-server integration. Block lists maintained in one path but not mirrored in the others leave gaps, so a creative blocked on one route can still clear on another. A standard worth enforcing is enforced everywhere the publisher transacts, not only in the path that happened to get the most attention.

A few signs point to block lists that do not match the publisher's standards:
  • The advertiser-domain and category block lists are empty or have not been reviewed against the publisher's current brand-safety policy
  • Category blocks are keyed to a deprecated IAB taxonomy version rather than the current Content Taxonomy
  • Blocking is maintained on one demand path but not mirrored across the others the publisher runs
  • The publisher cannot point to a written policy that the block lists are meant to reflect
You may want to confirm that the block lists are a current reflection of a written brand-safety standard. One approach is to reconcile the advertiser-domain, category, and creative-attribute blocks against that standard, then check that the same blocks are applied across every demand path the publisher runs through, including APS.

site_type_overlays.publishing: On a media site, the brand-safety standard is usually tied to editorial identity, so a news publisher may bar advertiser categories that conflict with its coverage and a lifestyle brand may bar categories that clash with its positioning. You may want to reconcile the category and advertiser-domain blocks against that editorial policy specifically, rather than relying on a generic default list that does not know what the publication stands for.
site_type_overlays.ecommerce: On a commerce-content site, the most sensitive blocks are usually direct competitors and categories that would undercut the site's own products. A competitor's creative running next to a product page can divert a reader who was close to a purchase. You may want to keep the competitor advertiser-domain list current as the retail landscape shifts, and confirm it is applied to the commercial inventory rather than only the editorial pages.

educate.learn_more_note: Amazon Publisher Services's detailed configuration documentation for setting advertiser and category block lists is in the partner portal (login required).

investigate.intro: Review the advertiser-domain, category, and creative-attribute block lists the publisher routes through Amazon Publisher Services (APS), and reconcile them against the publisher's written brand-safety standard and the demand paths running alongside APS.

aps_console.label: Amazon Publisher Services console
steps: Open the Amazon Publisher Services partner console and locate the brand-safety or blocking controls for the publisher's account.
Record the advertiser domains, ad categories, and creative attributes currently blocked, and note when each list was last reviewed.
Ask the publisher for its written brand-safety standard, then compare the blocked entries against it and note any advertiser or category the standard bars that the list does not.
Note which IAB content taxonomy version the category blocks are keyed to, and whether it matches the current Content Taxonomy.

cross_path_review.label: Cross-path block-list review
steps: List every demand path the publisher runs, e.g., Amazon Publisher Services, a Prebid wrapper, and any direct ad-server integration.
For each path, record the advertiser-domain and category blocks in force.
Compare the lists across paths and note any advertiser or category blocked on one path but allowed on another, which leaves a gap a blocked creative can clear through.

evaluate.logic: The practitioner records the advertiser-domain, category, and creative-attribute blocks routed through Amazon Publisher Services (APS), the date each list was last reviewed, the IAB taxonomy version the category blocks use, and whether the same blocks apply across the publisher's other demand paths. Block lists that match a written brand-safety standard, use the current taxonomy, and are mirrored across every demand path pass. Lists that are empty, stale, keyed to a deprecated taxonomy, or applied on one path but not the others are a fail recorded with the specific advertisers or categories the standard bars that the lists do not cover.
evaluate.fallback_message: Record the block lists, their last-reviewed dates, the taxonomy version, and the cross-path coverage above to see the evaluation.

generate.narrative_template: {{voice_found}} that the advertiser, category, and domain block lists {{site_name}} routes through Amazon Publisher Services (APS) do not match its brand-safety standard, so creatives the publisher means to refuse can clear in the auction. Because APS sends these blocking signals into the bid requests it makes to demand partners, the standard is enforced only to the extent the lists are populated and current, and a list that is empty, stale, or keyed to a deprecated IAB taxonomy version leaves gaps. {{voice_recommend}} reconciling the advertiser-domain, category, and creative-attribute blocks against a written brand-safety policy, moving category blocks onto the current Content Taxonomy, and mirroring the same blocks across every demand path the publisher runs. After the change, recheck which creatives clear to confirm the blocks hold.

audit_checks.title: Block lists are over-broad and suppress demand the publisher meant to keep
audit_checks.impact_rationale: Blocking protects the brand, but a list that is wider than the publisher's standard requires removes demand from the auction that the publisher would have accepted. Broad category blocks, duplicated entries left over from old campaigns, and domains blocked without a current reason thin the pool of bids, which lowers competition and the price each impression clears at without protecting anything the publisher actually cares about.

educate.base:
A block list has a cost on the revenue side as well as a benefit on the safety side. Every advertiser domain and every category a publisher blocks is demand removed from the auction Amazon Publisher Services (APS) runs, which means fewer bidders competing for the impression and, often, a lower clearing price. The benefit is real when the blocked demand is something the publisher genuinely refuses to run. The cost is wasted when the block is wider than the publisher's standard requires.

Over-broad blocking grows quietly through ordinary maintenance. A broad parent category gets blocked to keep out one narrow sub-category, taking acceptable demand down with it. Advertiser domains get added during a one-off dispute and never removed once the reason passes. Lists get copied between accounts or demand paths and accumulate entries no one can explain. None of these protect the brand. The publisher never objected to the demand they suppress, yet each one thins the pool of bids and trims the price the inventory clears at.

The IAB content taxonomy is built in tiers, so a publisher can usually block a specific sub-category rather than the whole parent. Choosing the narrowest block that still covers the publisher's standard keeps acceptable demand in the auction. The audit question is whether each block earns its place by reflecting a current standard, or whether the list has grown wider than it needs to be and is costing revenue for no brand-safety gain.

A few signs point to block lists that are over-broad:
  • Broad parent categories are blocked where a narrower sub-category would have covered the publisher's standard
  • Advertiser domains remain on the list with no current reason, e.g., left over from a resolved dispute or an old campaign
  • Duplicate or unexplained entries have accumulated from lists copied between accounts or demand paths
  • Reporting shows fill rate or average price lagging on inventory where the block list is widest
You may want to confirm that each block reflects a current standard rather than old residue. One approach is to review the widest blocks against the publisher's policy, narrow any parent-category block to the specific sub-category it was meant to cover, and remove domains whose reason no longer applies.

site_type_overlays.publishing: On a media site, broad category blocks can remove seasonal or event demand the publisher would have welcomed, e.g., a travel block kept year-round that suppresses demand the publication is happy to run outside a sensitive window. You may want to review whether any broad block can be narrowed to the specific sub-category or time window the standard actually requires.
site_type_overlays.ecommerce: On a commerce-content site, a competitor block list can grow to cover whole categories rather than the specific direct competitors the publisher means to bar, which removes adjacent demand that is not a real conflict. You may want to narrow the competitor block to the specific advertiser domains that compete with the site's own products, rather than blocking a broad retail category outright.

educate.learn_more_note: Amazon Publisher Services's reporting on demand affected by block lists is in the partner portal (login required).

investigate.intro: Review the breadth of the block lists routed through Amazon Publisher Services (APS) against the publisher's standard, and read fill rate and average price on the inventory where the lists are widest to see whether the blocks are costing demand the publisher meant to keep.

aps_console.label: Amazon Publisher Services console
steps: Open the Amazon Publisher Services partner console and list the blocked advertiser domains, categories, and creative attributes.
For each broad category block, note whether the publisher's standard required the whole parent category or only a narrower sub-category.
For each blocked advertiser domain, ask the publisher whether the reason for the block still applies, and flag any domain blocked without a current reason.
Note any duplicate or unexplained entries that appear to have accumulated from lists copied between accounts or demand paths.

aps_reporting.label: Amazon Publisher Services reporting
steps: Build a report broken out by the inventory affected by the widest block lists, with fill rate and average eCPM.
Compare that inventory against inventory with lighter blocking to see whether fill rate or average price lags where the blocks are widest.
Note any case where a broad block lines up with a drop in fill or price, which points to acceptable demand being suppressed for no brand-safety gain.

evaluate.logic: The practitioner records each broad block, whether the publisher's standard required that breadth or a narrower scope would have covered it, whether each blocked domain still has a current reason, and how fill rate and average price compare on the inventory where the blocks are widest. A block list where each entry reflects a current standard and the breadth is no wider than the standard requires passes. A list carrying broad parent-category blocks that a sub-category would cover, domains with no current reason, or duplicate residue, especially where reporting shows fill or price lagging, is a fail recorded with the specific entries that can be narrowed or removed.
evaluate.fallback_message: Record the breadth of each block, whether its reason still applies, and the fill and price on the affected inventory above to see the evaluation.

generate.narrative_template: {{voice_found}} that the block lists {{site_name}} routes through Amazon Publisher Services (APS) are wider than its brand-safety standard requires, so demand the publisher would have accepted is removed from the auction. Every blocked advertiser domain and category is a bidder taken out of the APS auction, which thins competition and trims the clearing price, and a broad parent-category block, a domain left over from a resolved dispute, or duplicate residue costs revenue without protecting anything the publisher objected to. {{voice_recommend}} reviewing the widest blocks against the written policy, narrowing each broad category block to the specific sub-category it was meant to cover, and removing domains whose reason no longer applies. After the change, recheck fill rate and average price on the affected inventory to confirm the demand returns.
